patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] perf record: Return the written size from process_comp_header()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] perf record: Return the written size from process_comp_header()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the process_comp_header() function in perf record to return the number of bytes written or -1 if it won't fit, instead of just returning an increment. The caller is then responsible for checking whether a record fits within the remaining space. This change allows the function to reject records that are too large and prevents potential buffer overflows.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 2/2] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 2/2] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes an issue where the perf record tool emits multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records when a single mmap push exceeds the maximum record size due to Zstd compression. The problem occurs because the header.size field is truncated, causing write failures and mis-framed data. To fix this, the patch changes the process_comp_header function to set per-record data_size, 8-byte-align header.size, and zero trailing padding. This ensures that each record is written correctly, even when compressed output exceeds the maximum record size.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 0/2] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 0/2] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a regression in the perf record command that causes it to abort recording and produce an invalid compressed file when using Zstd compression for multiple records. The fix involves returning the written size from process_comp_header() and adjusting the buffer check to prevent overrunning the output buffer. This allows multiple compressed records to be processed correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/2] syscall_user_dispatch: Make it configurable in Kconfig
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new Kconfig option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make the Syscall User Dispatch feature configurable. The feature allows a thread's system calls to be intercepted and redirected to a userspace signal handler based on a prctl() configured instruction pointer range. This change makes it possible for users to disable this feature independently of the GENERIC_SYSCALL option. The patch modifies several files, including Kconfig, entry-common.h, syscall_user_dispatch.h, and others, to add the new configuration option and update related code.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Price
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH is disabled and syscall_user_dispatch_clear_on_dispatch() is called, which can lead to a null pointer dereference.

Syscall User Dispatch is presently built under CONFIG_GENERIC_SYSCALL and
cannot be disabled independently.

Add CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH to make it an optional feature.

Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
Signed-off-by: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>

--- a/arch/Kconfig
+++ b/arch/Kconfig
@@ -114,6 +114,16 @@ config GENERIC_ENTRY
 	select GENERIC_IRQ_ENTRY
 	select GENERIC_SYSCALL
 
+config SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH
+	bool "Syscall User Dispatch"
+	depends on GENERIC_ENTRY
+	default y
+	help
+	  Syscall User Dispatch lets a thread have its own system calls
+	  intercepted and redirected to a userspace signal handler based
+	  on a prctl() configured instruction pointer range.
+	  If unsure, say Y.
+
 config KPROBES
 	bool "Kprobes"
 	depends on HAVE_KPROBES

 	 * of these syscalls is unknown.

 	if (work & SYSCALL_WORK_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH) {
-		if (unlikely(current->syscall_dispatch.on_dispatch)) {
-			current->syscall_dispatch.on_dispatch = false;
+		if (syscall_user_dispatch_clear_on_dispatch())
 			return;
-		}
 	}
 
 	audit_syscall_exit(regs);

 					    unsigned long len, char __user *selector)

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a sysctl (kernel.syscall_user_dispatch) to control whether userspace can arm syscall user dispatch via prctl or ptrace. The default value is 1, allowing arming of syscall user dispatch. Disabling it while a task has already armed it does not cause the task's dispatch to become inactive, but rather remains active until the user attempts to disable/re-enable it via prctl or ptrace. This approach prevents undefined behavior in userland when translating non-Linux syscalls as Linux syscalls.

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Price
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the sysctl for syscall_user_dispatch is not properly initialized, and requested to add a call to register_sysctl_init() in the late_initcall section.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the core/entry branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     34ef6308418f138395a664bf9f5d80ace9825ac6
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/34ef6308418f138395a664bf9f5d80ace9825ac6
Author:        Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
AuthorDate:    Mon, 06 Jul 2026 10:00:19 -04:00
Committer:     Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>
CommitterDate: Tue, 07 Jul 2026 23:03:17 +02:00

syscall_user_dispatch: Add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl

Add a matching sysctl to go with CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH.

kernel.syscall_user_dispatch (default 1 - allow) controls whether
userspace may arm syscall user dispatch (both via prctl and ptrace).

Disarming is always permitted - same semantics as comparable knobs.

Disabling while a task has armed syscall user dispatch does not
cause it to become inactive - instead it remains active until the
user attempts to disable/re-enable via prctl or ptrace.  On the
next attempt to re-enable, the prctl/ptrace call fails gracefully.

The alternative would cause programs translating non-linux syscalls
to interpret those syscalls as linux syscalls, resulting in undefined
userland behavior.

Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
Signed-off-by: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260706140020.873735-3-gourry@gourry.net
---
 Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst | 17 +++++++++-
 kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c        | 38 ++++++++++++++++----
 2 files changed, 48 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)

diff --git a/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst b/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst
index 9aed74e..2234644 100644
--- a/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst
+++ b/Documentation/admin-guide/sysctl/kernel.rst
@@ -1401,6 +1401,23 @@ Note that if you change this from 0 to 1, already created segments
 without users and with a dead originative process will be destroyed.
 
 
+syscall_user_dispatch
+=====================
+
+Controls whether userspace may arm Syscall User Dispatch via
+``prctl(PR_SET_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, ...)`` or the
+``PTRACE_SET_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH_CONFIG`` ptrace request:
+
+  ==  ===================================================================
+  0   Arming syscall user dispatch is denied with ``-EPERM``.  Tasks that
+      already armed it keep it, and disabling it is always permitted.
+  1   (default) Arming syscall user dispatch is permitted.
+  ==  ===================================================================
+
+Only present when the kernel is built with ``CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH``
+and ``CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL``.
+
+
 sysctl_writes_strict
 ====================
 
diff --git a/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c b/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
index d89dffc..310c72e 100644
--- a/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
+++ b/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
@@ -3,19 +3,20 @@
  * Copyright (C) 2020 Collabora Ltd.
  */
 
+#include <asm/syscall.h>
+#include <linux/elf.h>
 #include <linux/entry-common.h>
-#include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/prctl.h>
 #include <linux/ptrace.h>
-#include <linux/syscall_user_dispatch.h>
-#include <linux/uaccess.h>
-#include <linux/signal.h>
-#include <linux/elf.h>
-
+#include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/sched/signal.h>
 #include <linux/sched/task_stack.h>
+#include <linux/signal.h>
+#include <linux/syscall_user_dispatch.h>
+#include <linux/sysctl.h>
+#include <linux/uaccess.h>
 
-#include <asm/syscall.h>
+static bool syscall_user_dispatch_allowed __read_mostly = true;
 
 static void trigger_sigsys(struct pt_regs *regs)
 {
@@ -102,6 +103,10 @@ static int task_set_syscall_user_dispatch(struct task_struct *task, unsigned lon
 		return -EINVAL;
 	}
 
+	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
+	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
+		return -EPERM;
+
 	/*
 	 * access_ok() will clear memory tags for tagged addresses
 	 * if current has memory tagging enabled.
@@ -172,3 +177,22 @@ int syscall_user_dispatch_set_config(struct task_struct *task, unsigned long siz
 	return task_set_syscall_user_dispatch(task, cfg.mode, cfg.offset, cfg.len,
 					      (char __user *)(uintptr_t)cfg.selector);
 }
+
+#ifdef CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL
+static const struct ctl_table syscall_user_dispatch_sysctls[] = {
+	{
+		.procname	= "syscall_user_dispatch",
+		.data		= &syscall_user_dispatch_allowed,
+		.maxlen		= sizeof(syscall_user_dispatch_allowed),
+		.mode		= 0644,
+		.proc_handler	= proc_dobool,
+	},
+};
+
+static int __init syscall_user_dispatch_sysctl_init(void)
+{
+	register_sysctl_init("kernel", syscall_user_dispatch_sysctls);
+	return 0;
+}
+late_initcall(syscall_user_dispatch_sysctl_init);
+#endif /* CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL */

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make the Syscall User Dispatch feature configurable at both build and runtime. kernel.syscall_user_dispatch (default 1 - allow) controls whether
userspace may arm syscall user dispatch (both via prctl and ptrace).

Disarming is always permitted - same semantics as comparable knobs.

Disabling while a task has armed syscall user dispatch does not
cause it to become inactive - instead it remains active until the
user attempts to disable/re-enable via prctl or ptrace.  On the
next attempt to re-enable, the prctl/ptrace call fails gracefully.

userland behavior.

+  0   Arming syscall user dispatch is denied with ``-EPERM``.  Tasks that
+      already armed it keep it, and disabling it is always permitted.
+  1   (default) Arming syscall user dispatch is permitted.
+  ==  ===================================================================
+
+Only present when the kernel is built with ``CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH``
+and ``CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL``.
+
+

 ====================
 
diff --git a/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c b/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
index d89dffc..2002c7a 100644
--- a/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
+++ b/kernel/entry/syscall_user_dispatch.c
@@ -2,21 +2,22 @@

-#include <linux/sched.h>

-#include <linux/syscall_user_dispatch.h>
-#include <linux/uaccess.h>
-#include <linux/signal.h>
-#include <linux/elf.h>
-
+#include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/sched/signal.h>
 #include <linux/sched/task_stack.h>
+#include <linux/signal.h>
+#include <linux/syscall_user_dispatch.h>
+#include <linux/sysctl.h>
+#include <linux/uaccess.h>
 
 #include <asm/syscall.h>
 
+static bool syscall_user_dispatch_allowed __read_mostly = true;
+

 {
 	struct kernel_siginfo info;
@@ -102,6 +103,10 @@ static int task_set_syscall_user_dispatch(struct task_struct *task, unsigned lon
 		return -EINVAL;
 	}
 
+	/* Arming can be denied at runtime via sysctl, disarming is allowed */
+	if (mode != PR_SYS_DISPATCH_OFF && !syscall_user_dispatch_allowed)
+		return -EPERM;
+
 	/*
 	 * access_ok() will clear memory tags for tagged addresses
 	 * if current has memory tagging enabled.
@@ -172,3 +177,22 @@ int syscall_user_dispatch_set_config(struct task_struct *task, unsigned long siz
 	return task_set_syscall_user_dispatch(task, cfg.mode, cfg.offset, cfg.len,
 					      (char __user *)(uintptr_t)cfg.selector);
 }
+
+#ifdef CONFIG_PROC_SYSCTL
+static const struct ctl_table syscall_user_dispatch_sysctls[] = {
+	{
+		.procname	= "syscall_user_dispatch",

+		.mode		= 0644,
+		.proc_handler	= proc_dobool,
+	},
+};

+static int __init syscall_user_dispatch_sysctl_init(void)
+{

+	return 0;
+}

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/5] sunrpc: route to a populated pool in svc_pool_for_cpu()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the `svc_pool_for_cpu()` function in the sunrpc module to route incoming RPC requests to a populated pool, rather than one that is currently idle. This ensures that all incoming requests are serviced, even if some pools have no threads assigned to them. The change trades off NUMA locality for guaranteed service of incoming requests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/5] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch hardcodes the pool mode to pernode, removing other modes from the sunrpc code. The default behavior on multi-NUMA hosts changes to one pool per NUMA node, which is considered the right choice everywhere. This change simplifies the code by removing mode-based switch statements and ensures proper thread affinity and NUMA-local memory allocation. The patch also updates documentation and preserves backward compatibility for module parameters and netlink interfaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/5] sunrpc: guarantee a thread per pool when auto-distributing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that when distributing threads across multiple pools in the sunrpc service, no pool is left empty even if the total number of threads requested is less than the number of pools. This is achieved by guaranteeing at least one thread per pool, which may result in a total number of threads exceeding the requested count. The change only affects the auto-distribution path and does not impact explicit per-pool thread counts set via svc_set_pool_threads().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/5] sunrpc: tear down pool counters before dropping the pool map reference
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential memory leak in the sunrpc service by reordering the destruction of pool counters and the dropping of the pool map reference. The original order caused issues when the last pooled service was destroyed, as it would skip destroying the percpu counters. The patch ensures that the pool counters are destroyed while the pool map is still referenced, preventing a memory leak.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/5] sunrpc: derive the pool count instead of caching it in sv_nrpools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the cached field sv_nrpools from svc_serv and instead derives the pool count using a new helper function svc_serv_nrpools(). The function uses the existing fields sv_is_pooled and svc_pool_map.npools to calculate the correct number of pools. This change is made possible by the fact that the pool mode is always per-node, making it safe to read npools without holding the mutex. The patch also removes a modulus operation from svc_pool_for_cpu(), which should improve performance.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about increasing the interface surface between sunrpc.ko and nfsd.ko by exporting struct svc_pool_map, instead choosing to keep the interface small with a single accessor function.

Doing what you suggest would increase the interface "surface" between
sunrpc.ko and nfsd.ko.

Currently struct svc_pool_map is only defined in net/sunrpc/svc.c, so
we'd not only need to export the symbol, but also we'd have to make
that definition public.

Why expose so many internal details to other modules when all we need
is this one accessor? I think keeping this interface small is
preferable.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that removing the cached field and replacing it with a helper function to derive the pool count has an abstract benefit (interface size) but may have a concrete cost (code size), and requested further consideration of this trade-off.

In general, yes.  But it is not without cost.  Sometimes the cost

Interface size has an abstract value.  Code size can have concrete
value.  Comparing them is subjective I guess.
I have no concrete evidence that there is a problem, so I'll stop
objecting.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series hardcodes the pool mode to per-node, removing other modes and addressing issues such as scheduling RPCs to pools with no threads. It also implements suggestions from Neil to eliminate the serv.sv_nrpools field, which has the side effect of reducing the modulus operations in the svc_pool_for_cpu() hotpath. The patches include fixes for comment cleanups, shortcoming of existing code, and auto-thread-placement case. The approach taken is to ensure that each populated node has at least one thread and prevent routing requests to empty pools.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/3] fuse: don't clear folio uptodate on writethrough errors
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a potential security issue in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation, specifically in the writethrough path where write errors can lead to folios being marked as uptodate, potentially exposing sensitive information to userspace. The patch modifies the fuse_send_write_pages() function to avoid clearing the uptodate flag on folios when a write error occurs.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential security issue in the FUSE implementation and suggested modifying the fuse_send_write_pages() function to avoid clearing the uptodate flag on folios when a write error occurs.
review_comments.raw_body:
Looks good:

Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch and indicated agreement with its changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, 8 Jul 2026 at 00:05, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> In the writethrough path (fuse_send_write_pages()), if the write to the
> server failed or was a short write, the uptodate flag on the folios are
> cleared.
>
> As explained by Matthew in [1], this is dangerous because the folio may
> be mapped into userspace. The mm code has the invariant that a
> non-uptodate folio must never be visible to userspace (to avoid
> potentially leaking confidental information to userspace) and has checks
> in place for this that if violated can bring down the whole machine.
>
> Practically speaking, the effect of this change for the fuse
> writethrough error path is that if an application does a write and then
> the server fails to persist the data or only services a short write, the
> page cache folio keeps the data the application wrote instead of being
> reverted to the server's contents on the next read. The failure is still
> reported to the application synchronously through the short count /
> error return of the write() syscall. Folios that were only partially
> written are unaffected since they were never marked uptodate in the
> first place (fuse_fill_write_page() only marks a folio as uptodate if
> the whole folio was written to).
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/ajtPMgO65FA1TXhi@casper.infradead.org/
>
> Suggested-by: Matthew Wilcox <willy@infradead.org>
> Reviewed-by: Darrick J. Wong <djwong@kernel.org>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>

Acked-by: Miklos Szeredi <mszeredi@redhat.com>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/2] mm/huge_memory: extend thp_underused() to mTHP folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends the thp_underused() function to handle mTHP folios by scaling the khugepaged_max_ptes_none sysctl value proportionally to the size of the folio, allowing for more efficient splitting of underused large memory pages.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged a concern about integer division truncation affecting small mTHP folios and plans to fix it in the next version.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jul 7, 2026 at 1:18 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> thp_underused() decides whether a large folio on the deferred split
> list is underused and should be split so that its zero subpages can be
> reclaimed. However, the logic in it only accounts for PMD-sized folios.
>
> As preparatory work for splitting underused mTHP folios, make the logic
> in thp_underused() compatible with mTHP-sized folios. This uses the
> existing khugepaged_max_ptes_none sysctl value and scales it
> proportionally to the size of the folio as the threshold for how many
> zero-filled pages a folio may contain before it's considered underused.
>
> This introduces no functional changes for PMD-size folios.
>
> Suggested-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
> ---
>  mm/huge_memory.c | 12 ++++++++----
>  1 file changed, 8 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
> index c0892cc533a9..ea5c350b4818 100644
> --- a/mm/huge_memory.c
> +++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
> @@ -4413,24 +4413,28 @@ static unsigned long deferred_split_count(struct shrinker *shrink,
>  static bool thp_underused(struct folio *folio)
>  {
>         int num_zero_pages = 0, num_filled_pages = 0;
> +       int nr_pages = folio_nr_pages(folio);
> +       unsigned int max_ptes_none;
>         int i;
>
> -       if (khugepaged_max_ptes_none == HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1)
> +       max_ptes_none = khugepaged_max_ptes_none * nr_pages / HPAGE_PMD_NR;
> +
> +       if (max_ptes_none >= nr_pages - 1)
>                 return false;

Andrew kindly directed me to a comment by Sashiko [1]:

"Does this scale calculation unintentionally disable underused splitting for
small mTHPs with valid sysctl settings?
Due to integer division truncation, max_ptes_none >= nr_pages - 1 can match
configurations that aren't the sysctl sentinel (HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1).
...
Should the sentinel check remain against the raw
khugepaged_max_ptes_none value before scaling?"

Sashiko is right. I'll fix this up in v2 and keep the original
khugepaged_max_ptes_none == HPAGE_PMD_NR - 1 check on the raw
non-scaled value.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260707201735.4113107-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/2] mm/memory: add anonymous mTHP folios to deferred split list
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a performance issue on ARM systems by adding anonymous mTHP folios to the deferred split list, allowing for more efficient memory management and reducing TLB misses.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Identified potential scalability issues with adding all large anon folios to the deferred split queue and suggested making smarter decisions about what to scan/reclaim first.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Proposed a potential solution to add mTHPs only for which the policy is set to always, and suggested that this approach could bring performance on par with x86.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 08/07/2026 08:56, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 7/7/26 22:17, Joanne Koong wrote:
>> Unlike for PMD-sized folios, an anonymous mTHP folio doesn't get added
>> to the deferred split list at fault or collapse time. As a result, a
>> fully mapped mTHP folio that is mostly zero-filled doesn't get split by
>> the deferred split shrinker when the system is under memory pressure.
>>
>> Add anonymous mTHP folios to the deferred split list so that if there's
>> memory pressure, a zero-filled mTHP can be split with its zero pages
>> remapped to the shared zero page and then reclaimed.
>>
>> To minimize overhead on the common order-0 fault path, the
>> deferred_split_folio() call is guarded by an inline folio_test_large()
>> check.
>>
>> Suggested-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
>> Signed-off-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>
>> ---
>>  mm/memory.c | 2 ++
>>  1 file changed, 2 insertions(+)
>>
>> diff --git a/mm/memory.c b/mm/memory.c
>> index 6637c5b13c9b..441d918e3dc0 100644
>> --- a/mm/memory.c
>> +++ b/mm/memory.c
>> @@ -5259,6 +5259,8 @@ void map_anon_folio_pte_nopf(struct folio *folio, pte_t *pte,
>>  	folio_add_lru_vma(folio, vma);
>>  	set_ptes(vma->vm_mm, addr, pte, entry, nr_pages);
>>  	update_mmu_cache_range(NULL, vma, addr, pte, nr_pages);
>> +	if (folio_test_large(folio))
>> +		deferred_split_folio(folio, false);
>>  }
>>  
>>  static void map_anon_folio_pte_pf(struct folio *folio, pte_t *pte,
> 
> I had a session [1] at LSF/MM about having essentially all large anon folios
> part of the the deferred split queue.
> 
> (1) I don't think this scales.
> 

What if we only add the mTHPs for which the policy is set to always.

I think it doesn't scale if someone sets all orders to always, but if you only
set the 2M mTHP sysfs to always, it should be the same as what we have today
for PMD order?

The main motivation for this series is to try and bring the performance for
ARM on par with x86. One of the differences is TLB misses. I imagine the lower
churn in kernel by using larger page sizes will help as well. But we will
run into OOMs without the shrinker.
 

> (2) I suspect the shrinker should make smarter decisions of what to scan/reclaim
> first.

Yes definitely agree!

Alexandre is looking into this as well.

> 
> I think this needs more proper thought.
> 
> [1]
> https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1RfKWCY1AMVns-WLn-QdAWbI2a-rA7fbFyh7XD1Wn5BY/edit?usp=sharing
>

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a memory regression issue caused by not reclaiming unused portions of 2M THP folios on ARM, which can occur when deploying 2M THP=always. The patches modify the thp_underused() function to work with non-PMD-sized folios and add anonymous mTHP folios to the deferred split list from the anonymous fault/collapse path, allowing the shrinker to find and split them. This approach is compatible with existing PMD THP collapse thresholds and ensures that unused portions of 2M THPs can be broken down and reclaimed, preventing memory regressions.

patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch separates the ext4 iomap begin function into two separate functions: one for read operations and one for write operations. This is done to reduce the size of the ext4_read_iomap_begin function, which was found to be larger than xfs's equivalent function due to being a combined read/write begin function. The separation aims to improve performance by reducing frontend stalls and icache misses. The patch also includes a test suggestion to verify if this change improves ext4 performance.

review_comments.author: changfengnan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer changfengnan ran two rounds of tests and found that the original patch caused a 1.5% performance drop in ext4, which they attributed to too many inline functions expanding the filesystem read_iter hot path and increasing frontend/register pressure. They made changes by reverting iomap_dio_rw() and iomap_dio_simple() to non-inline versions, which restored normal performance.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 01/15] mm: decouple protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the protnone helpers from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING by introducing a new configuration option, CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE. This allows architectures that support present-but-inaccessible page table entries (protnone PTEs) to use this capability independently of NUMA balancing. The six supported architectures are updated to select the new option, and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING now depends on it. No functional changes are made, as the same set of architectures continues to have working protnone support.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 02/15] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE bit macros to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the uffd-wp PTE bit macros to simply 'uffd' across various architectures, including x86 and arm64. The rename is a mechanical change only, without any changes in behavior. This is done because the uffd-wp bit will soon have an additional consumer, userfaultfd RWP, which uses it for access-tracking PTEs. The VMA flag determines the WP vs RWP semantics of this bit, and the rename reflects that the bit simply indicates that userfaultfd has claimed the entry.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 03/15] mm: rename uffd-wp PTE accessors to uffd
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames kernel internal names related to the uffd-wp PTE bit, which is reused for marking access-tracking PTEs alongside write-protected ones. The renaming affects various header files and source code in the Linux kernel, including pgtable.h, huge_memory.c, hugetlb.c, and userfaultfd.c. The changes are purely mechanical and do not alter any behavior. The patch also updates ftrace strings to keep existing trace-based tooling compatible.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 04/15] userfaultfd: test uffd VMA flags through the vma_flags_t API
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the userfaultfd VMA flag helpers to use the vma_flags_t accessor function, which is the recommended API for accessing these flags in a way that's aware of configuration-gated per-mode masks. The change does not introduce any functional changes, as the same bits are being read and the masks fold to the same code as before. This patch is part of a larger series of updates to userfaultfd.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 05/15] mm: add VM_UFFD_RWP VMA flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new VMA flag called VM_UFFD_RWP to the Linux kernel, which enables read-write protection for userfaultfd (Uffd) ranges. This extends the existing write-protection feature of Uffd to also trap reads on protected ranges. The flag is gated by CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP and is currently not set or queried anywhere in the codebase. The patch introduces new helpers and updates various files, including documentation, to support this new feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 06/15] mm: add MM_CP_UFFD_RWP change_protection() flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new flags to the change_protection() function in the Linux kernel: MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP flag is used for userfaultfd RWP protection, which sets a PTE to PAGE_NONE with the uffd bit set, making it distinguishable from other faults. The MM_CP_UFFD_RWP_RESOLVE flag is used to restore the original vma->vm_page_prot and clear the uffd bit after RWP protection has been applied. This patch also updates several functions in the kernel to use these new flags, including change_pte_range(), change_huge_pmd(), and hugetlb_change_protection(). The goal of this patch is to provide a consistent way for userfaultfd RWP protection across different memory types, such as anonymous, shared memory, transparent huge pages, and hugetlb.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 07/15] mm: preserve RWP marker across PTE rewrites
series_items.patch_summary: This patch ensures that the RWP (Read-Only Write-Protection) marker is preserved across PTE rewrites in various kernel paths, including swap, device-exclusive, migration, fork, and mprotect operations. The goal is to prevent the silent loss of this marker, which can lead to incorrect behavior when tracking page accesses. To achieve this, the patch modifies several functions to restore the PAGE_NONE semantics when necessary, effectively preserving the RWP bit across PTE rewrites.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 08/15] mm: handle VM_UFFD_RWP in khugepaged, rmap, and GUP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch extends three mm paths outside the fault handler gate to treat VM_UFFD_RWP (User Faultfd Read-Write Protected) the same as VM_UFFD_WP (User Faultfd Write Protected). The changes affect khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. In khugepaged, ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit are now protected from collapse or retraction. In rmap, batching is capped at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP to ensure each PTE is handled individually. In GUP, a fault is forced on VMAs with VM_UFFD_RWP regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. This ensures that RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker rather than for NUMA hinting.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 09/15] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT plumbing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the Linux kernel's userfaultfd system, allowing userspace to track read-write protection (RWP) on memory ranges. It introduces two new ioctls: UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP and UFFDIO_RWPROTECT, which enable RWP tracking and allow installing or removing RWP on a range. The feature is gated by CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP, which is automatically selected for 64-bit kernels with certain architecture features. The patch also includes documentation updates and changes to the userfaultfd code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 10/15] mm/userfaultfd: add RWP fault delivery and expose UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds read-write protection (RWP) fault delivery to the userfaultfd interface, allowing userspace to track and handle RWP faults. It introduces new functions to handle PTE and PMD faults, as well as hugetlb faults, and exposes a new UAPI command to register for RWP mode. The patch also updates various kernel files to support RWP fault delivery and userfaultfd interface changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 11/15] mm/pagemap: add PAGE_IS_ACCESSED for RWP tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new category to the pagemap, called PAGE_IS_ACCESSED, which is used for read-write protection (RWP) tracking in userfaultfd. It introduces a new bit in the PTE that indicates whether a page has been accessed, and scopes it to the VMA whose registered mode makes the bit meaningful. This change allows RWP to have its own pagemap category, distinct from write protection, and enables future implementations to move RWP to a separate PTE bit without affecting existing code. The patch also updates documentation and code to reflect these changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 12/15] userfaultfd: add UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC for async fault resolution
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the userfaultfd system, called UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC, which allows for asynchronous resolution of read-write page faults. This feature is designed to work in conjunction with working-set tracking and does not require the delivery of a message to resolve the fault. Instead, the kernel restores access to the pages in-place, allowing the faulting thread to continue without blocking. The patch also includes updates to various kernel files to support this new feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 13/15] userfaultfd: add UFFDIO_SET_MODE for runtime sync/async toggle
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new ioctl, UFFDIO_SET_MODE, which allows a VMM to toggle between sync and async RWP modes at runtime without re-registering the userfaultfd. The ioctl takes an enable/disable bitmask of UFFD_FEATURE_* flags, with only UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC being toggleable today. When switching to async mode, pending sync waiters are woken so they retry and auto-resolve under the new mode. This allows for dynamic switching between modes, enabling scenarios such as working set scanning in async mode followed by fault interception during page eviction in sync mode.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 14/15] selftests/mm: add userfaultfd RWP tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds userfaultfd read-write protect (RWP) tests to the Linux kernel's selftest suite. The tests cover various scenarios, including asynchronous and synchronous RWP modes, page mapping, mprotect, GUP walks, and fork operations. The tests verify that RWP works correctly in different memory types, such as anonymous, shared memory, and huge pages. The patch also includes a test to ensure that registering with both write-protect and read-write protect modes returns an error.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v10 15/15] Documentation/userfaultfd: document RWP working set tracking
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's admin guide on userfaultfd (UFFD) working set tracking, specifically covering read-write protection (RWP). It explains the sync and async fault models, UFFDIO_RWPROTECT semantics, and UFFD_FEATURE_RWP_ASYNC. The documentation also covers a typical virtual machine (VMM) workflow for working-set-tracking from detection to eviction and back to asynchronous mode.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support to userfaultfd (a Linux kernel interface) for tracking the working set of virtual machine guest memory, allowing a Virtual Machine Manager (VMM) to identify hot pages and reclaim cold ones to slower storage. The approach taken is to introduce a new registration mode, UFFDIO_REGISTER_MODE_RWP, which uses the same mechanism as existing modes but with additional functionality for working set tracking. This enables the VMM to detect which pages are still being accessed, safely reclaim cold pages, and fetch them back on demand when needed. The patch series includes 15 patches that address various issues and conflicts, including rebasing onto mm-new, resolving a conflict with a hugetlb swap-corruption fix, and tolerating EINVAL from madvise(MADV_NOHUGEPAGE). The patches have been tested and show no regressions in the selftests/mm/uffd-unit-tests.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the decoupling of protnone PTE infrastructure from NUMA balancing, agreeing to introduce CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE and make it independent of NUMA balancing. The existing architectures that support protnone PTEs will continue to have working protnone support, but the infrastructure is now available independently of NUMA balancing.

From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

pte_protnone() and pmd_protnone() detect present-but-inaccessible page
table entries. This capability is useful beyond NUMA balancing -- for
example, userfaultfd working set tracking uses protnone PTEs to track
page access without unmapping pages.

Introduce CONFIG_ARCH_HAS_PTE_PROTNONE to decouple the protnone PTE
infrastructure from CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING. The six architectures that
support protnone PTEs (x86_64, arm64, powerpc, s390, riscv, loongarch)
now select this option, and CONFIG_NUMA_BALANCING depends on it.

No functional change -- the same set of architectures continues to have
working protnone support, but the infrastructure is now available
independently of NUMA balancing.

review_comments.raw_body:
From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

Preparatory patch for userfaultfd read-write protection (RWP). RWP
extends userfaultfd protection from plain write-protection (WP) to
full read-write protection: accesses to an RWP-protected range --
reads as well as writes -- trap through userfaultfd.

Reserve VM_UFFD_RWP, add the userfaultfd_rwp() and
userfaultfd_protected() helpers, and wire up the smaps "ur" entry and
the trace-flag table the rest of the series will use. The flag is
gated on CONFIG_USERFAULTFD_RWP, which is introduced together with the
UAPI in a later patch; until then VM_UFFD_RWP aliases VM_NONE and
every downstream check folds to dead code.

Nothing sets or queries the flag yet.

Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6

     um    userfaultfd missing tracking

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the change_protection() primitive used by userfaultfd RWP, explaining that an RWP-protected PTE is PAGE_NONE with the uffd PTE bit set and that MM_CP_UFFD_RWP and MM_CP_UFFD_WP share the same PTE bit. The author confirmed that they will use this primitive in their patch series.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern that the uffd PTE bit must survive any kernel path that rewrites a PTE on a VM_UFFD_RWP VMA, otherwise the marker carrying PAGE_NONE semantics is silently dropped and the next access leaks past RWP tracking. The author explained how they plan to wire preservation through every such path in their patch.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about silent destruction or bypassing of per-PTE RWP state in three mm paths: khugepaged, rmap, and GUP. The author agreed to extend each path to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same as VM_UFFD_WP, ensuring that RWP state is preserved.
review_comments.raw_body:
From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>

Three mm paths outside the fault handler gate on the uffd PTE bit
today: khugepaged (skip collapse on ranges carrying markers), rmap
(cap unmap batching), and GUP (force a fault through
gup_can_follow_protnone). Extend each to treat VM_UFFD_RWP the same
as VM_UFFD_WP; otherwise per-PTE RWP state is silently destroyed or
bypassed.

khugepaged: try_collapse_pte_mapped_thp() and
file_backed_vma_is_retractable() already refuse to collapse or
retract page tables on ranges carrying the uffd PTE bit. Broaden the
VMA predicate from userfaultfd_wp() to userfaultfd_protected() so
VM_UFFD_RWP ranges get the same protection. hpage_collapse_scan_pmd()
needs no change \u2014 its existing pte_uffd() check already catches an
RWP PTE because it carries the uffd bit.

rmap: folio_unmap_pte_batch() caps batching at 1 for VM_UFFD_RWP so
the restore path handles each PTE with its own marker.

GUP: gup_can_follow_protnone() forces a fault on VM_UFFD_RWP VMAs
regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT. RWP uses protnone as an
access-tracking marker, not for NUMA hinting, so any GUP \u2014 read or
write \u2014 must go through the userfaultfd fault path.

Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau <kas@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-6
Acked-by: Mike Rapoport (Microsoft) <rppt@kernel.org>
Reviewed-by: Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org>
---

 mm/rmap.c          |  2 +-
 3 files changed, 27 insertions(+), 9 deletions(-)

  * Indicates whether GUP can follow a PROT_NONE mapped page, or whether
- * a (NUMA hinting) fault is required.
+ * a (NUMA hinting or userfaultfd RWP) fault is required.
  */
 static inline bool gup_can_follow_protnone(const struct vm_area_struct *vma,
 					   unsigned int flags)
 {
+	/*
+	 * VM_UFFD_RWP uses protnone as an access-tracking marker, not for
+	 * NUMA hinting. GUP must always take a fault so the access is
+	 * delivered to userfaultfd, regardless of FOLL_HONOR_NUMA_FAULT.
+	 *
+	 * Only do so while the VMA is accessible. If it has been made
+	 * inaccessible (e.g. mprotect(PROT_NONE)), fall through to the guard
+	 * below: forcing a fault there would loop, as handle_mm_fault() makes
+	 * no progress on protnone in an inaccessible VMA, and the access is
+	 * denied regardless of RWP anyway.
+	 */
+	if (vma_test_single_mask(vma, VMA_UFFD_RWP) && vma_is_accessible(vma))
+		return false;
+
 	/*
 	 * If callers don't want to honor NUMA hinting faults, no need to
 	 * determine if we would actually have to trigger a NUMA hinting fault.

+++ b/mm/khugepaged.c

 		return SCAN_VMA_CHECK;
 
-	/* Keep pmd pgtable for uffd-wp; see comment in retract_page_tables() */
-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))
+	/*
+	 * Keep pmd pgtable while the uffd bit is in use; see comment in

+	 */

 		return SCAN_PTE_UFFD;

@@ -2107,13 +2110,14 @@ static bool file_backed_vma_is_retractable(struct vm_area_struct *vma)

 	 * the page table because there may be pte markers installed.
-	 * Other vmas can still have the same file mapped hugely, but
-	 * skip this one: it will always be mapped in small page size
-	 * for uffd-wp registered ranges.
+	 * VM_UFFD_RWP ranges similarly rely on per-PTE uffd state
+	 * and cannot be recycled to a shared PMD. Other vmas can still
+	 * have the same file mapped hugely, but skip this one: it will
+	 * always be mapped in small page size for these registrations.
 	 */
-	if (userfaultfd_wp(vma))
+	if (userfaultfd_protected(vma))

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes

version_history.subject: [PATCH] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes
[PATCH v2] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the PAGEMAP_SCAN functionality of the Linux kernel, specifically when dealing with PMD holes (large page table entries). The issue arises when an anonymous huge page is write-protected and then cleared using MADV_DONTNEED. As a result, the range is not reported as written, leading to stale data in subsequent incremental dumps. To fix this, the patch adds the PAGE_IS_WRITTEN category to the evaluation of holes in non-hugetlb uffd-wp VMAs, ensuring that PMD holes are correctly reported as written. A selftest has also been added to verify the fix.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/hugetlb: fix swap entry corruption when clearing uffd-wp at fork()

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/hugetlb: fix swap entry corruption when clearing uffd-wp at fork()

patches_submitted.patch_summary: The patch fixes a swap entry corruption issue when clearing the uffd-wp bit at fork() in hugetlb pages. The problem arises from the different handling of the uffd-wp bit in migration and hwpoison entries, leading to incorrect clear operations. The patch corrects this by using pte_swp_clear_uffd_wp() for migration entries and removing the unnecessary clear operation on hwpoison entries.
